topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon sun cast long, accusing shadows across the linoleum floor of my tiny apartment. I hadn't moved much all day, just existing in a low-grade fog of inadequacy. When I finally dragged myself to the mailbox, the act felt monumental, a performance I wasn't sure I was qualified for. Tucked amongst the junk mail and bills, something thick and creamy caught my eye. It was a card, unopened, the paper slightly softened at the edges. My fingers, usually clumsy, trembled as I pulled it out. On the front, in looping, elegant script, were the words, "Happy Birthday." My breath hitched. Who? Why? A sudden, sharp ache bloomed behind my ribs, a familiar, heavy pressure. I unfolded it slowly, my gaze skimming over the blank space where a name should have been. It felt like a misplaced spotlight, illuminating the vast emptiness inside me. I folded it back up instantly, shoving it deep into the pocket of my worn cardigan, desperate to bury the unexpected flicker of something resembling connection.

[story 2]

I stared at the card until the edges blurred. It was sitting on my kitchen counter, mocking me with its simple existence. Every surface in the room seemed to vibrate with the silence of my own unimportance. I picked it up, the texture of the card surprisingly smooth against my fingertips. I read the message aloud, the sound thin and reedy in the quiet kitchen. "Thinking of you." The phrase landed like a small stone in my stomach, jarring and unwelcome. I immediately felt a defensive tightening around my jaw. It was a gesture, a fleeting acknowledgment from someone I probably didn't even know, and the sheer effort of processing it felt exhausting. I dropped the card back onto the counter, the sound barely audible. I walked to the window, watching the indifferent city traffic flow below, feeling utterly disconnected, as if I were watching a performance I was never invited to attend.

[story 3]

The metallic clang of the mailbox door echoed in the hallway, a sound that seemed too loud for the hollow feeling settling in my chest. I retrieved the envelope, my movements sluggish, weighted down by a profound sense of unmeritedness. Inside, the card lay waiting. It was pristine, untouched by any stamp or address. My heart began to thump a frantic rhythm against my ribs, a panicked drum solo in the quiet room. I picked it up, my hand shaking so badly I almost dropped it again. There, centered on the card, was a birthday greeting. A name, elegantly written, someone else's name. A strange warmth, sharp and unwelcome, sparked deep within me, followed immediately by a cold wave of self-reproach. Why would someone think of me? I quickly smoothed the card flat on the table, trying to flatten the strange, unsettling sensation bubbling up inside, resolving to discard the whole thing before it could disrupt the fragile, dull equilibrium I had managed to maintain.
